message.content:
THE SYNTHESIS PROBLEM specifically — how does the published literature produce faithful CROSS-SOURCE
INFERENCE in generated reports?

Our core constraint: POLARIS verifies every sentence against a source span (entailment judge; a sentence introducing
"a fact, entity, MECHANISM ... NOT present in the SPAN" is NEUTRAL => DELETED). That is exactly the sentence type
that earns insight. We are considering a two-tier contract: FACT (span-grounded) + INTERPRETATION (hedged, no new
numbers/entities, no citations, contradiction-screened).

Search the literature and GitHub for: attribution/grounding frameworks that admit INFERENCE (not just extraction);
"inference-level attribution", "reasoning attribution", claim-level vs inference-level provenance; RAG faithfulness
work that separates EXTRACTIVE from ABSTRACTIVE claims; multi-document summarisation with cross-document reasoning;
hallucination-safe synthesis; NLI-based verification of inferences from MULTIPLE premises (rather than one span);
"entailment from a set of premises" verification. Also: how do systematic-review/meta-analysis tools handle this?

WHAT WE NEED: prior art (or a proof that none exists) for admitting a cross-source inference sentence while
GUARANTEEING no fabrication. If our two-tier contract is a known pattern, find its name and its failure modes. If it
is genuinely novel, say so — that changes how carefully we must test it.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

attributable to identified sources AIS framework inference extractive abstractive claims attribution

content.content:
Web search results for query: ""inference-level attribution" OR "reasoning attribution" grounding LLM generated text"

Links: [{"title":"Inferring Latent Intentions: Attributional Natural Language Inference in LLM Agents","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2601.08742"},{"title":"SAFE: Improving LLM Systems using Sentence-Level In-generation Attribution","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.12621"},{"title":"GitHub - HITsz-TMG/awesome-llm-attributions: A Survey of Attributions for Large Language Models · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/HITsz-TMG/awesome-llm-attributions"},{"title":"Attribution and Citation: Sourcing LLM Outputs - Interactive | Michael Brenndoerfer | Michael Brenndoerfer","url":"https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/attribution-and-citation"},{"title":"Multimodal Fact-Level Attribution for Verifiable Reasoning","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.11509"},{"title":"Rethinking Failure Attribution in Multi-Agent Systems: A Multi-Perspective Benchmark and Evaluation","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.25001"},{"title":"The Knowledge-Reasoning Dissociation: Fundamental Limitations of LLMs in Clinical Natural Language Inference","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2508.10777"},{"title":"Inferring Latent Intentions: Attributional Natural Language Inference in LLM Agents","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2601.08742"},{"title":"A Large Language Model Based Pipeline for Review of Systems Entity Recognition from Clinical Notes","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2506.11067"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found on inference-level attribution and reasoning attribution for grounding LLM-generated text:

## Key Concepts

Attribution can be split into two categories based on when source information is accessed: at training or inference time, with this work focusing on inference-time attribution (such as in Retrieval Augmented Generation systems).

Attribution refers to the capacity of a model to generate and provide evidence, often in the form of references or citations, that substantiates the claims or statements it produces.

## Reasoning Attribution and Grounding

Outputs that are verifiable and grounded in a model's input sources are particularly important when problems require multi-step reasoning, which amplifies the risk of hallucinations from propagating errors, and when producing long-form responses.

In multi-perspective failure attribution tasks, LLMs generate explicit attribution reasoning including failure reasons and ideal actions.

## Training and Implementation

Producing reliable attribution is not just an inference-time problem; it requires models trained to attribute their claims, with the primary approach being supervised fine-tuning on attributed corpora.

The search results show an active research area focused on making LLM outputs more verifiable and trustworthy through various attribution mechanisms at both training and inference stages.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Web search results for query: "attributable to identified sources AIS framework inference extractive abstractive claims attribution"

Links: [{"title":"Measuring Attribution in Natural Language Generation Models | Computational Linguistics | MIT Press","url":"https://direct.mit.edu/coli/article/49/4/777/116438/Measuring-Attribution-in-Natural-Language"},{"title":"Attribution and Citation: Sourcing LLM Outputs - Interactive | Michael Brenndoerfer | Michael Brenndoerfer","url":"https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/attribution-and-citation"},{"title":"Measuring Attribution in Natural Language Generation Models | alphaXiv","url":"https://www.alphaxiv.org/overview/2112.12870v2"},{"title":"Evaluating Evidence Attribution in Generated Fact Checking Explanations","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2406.12645"},{"title":"ClaimVer: Explainable Claim-Level Verification and Evidence Attribution   of Text Through Knowledge Graphs","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2403.09724"},{"title":"AIS/README.md at main · google-research-datasets/AIS","url":"https://github.com/google-research-datasets/AIS/blob/main/README.md"},{"title":"Extractable Content: How to Structure Pages AI Engines Cite","url":"https://authoritytech.io/blog/extractable-content-structure-ai-citations-2026"},{"title":"GitHub - google-research-datasets/AIS: AIS is an evaluation framework for assessing whether the output of natural language models only contains information about the external world that is verifiable in source documents, or \"Attributable to Identified Sources\". · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/google-research-datasets/AIS"},{"title":"A Survey of Large Language Models Attribution","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2311.03731"},{"title":"Cited but Not Verified: Parsing and Evaluating Source Attribution in LLM Deep Research Agents","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.06635"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the AIS framework and related concepts:

## AIS Framework Overview

The Attributable to Identified Sources (AIS) framework stipulates that NLG output pertaining to the external world is to be verified against an independent, provided source. More specifically, AIS is an evaluation framework for assessing whether the output of natural language models only contains information about the external world that is verifiable in source documents.

## Application to Different Tasks

AIS has been validated on generation datasets spanning three tasks including conversational QA datasets, a summarization dataset, and a table-to-text dataset. AIS is crucial in summarization where a generated summary must be well-supported by the source article.

## Extractive vs. Abstractive Performance

On the CNN/DM dataset, extractive models significantly outperformed abstractive models in AIS scores, aligning with expectations that direct copying reduces hallucination risk.

## Attribution and Natural Language Inference

The AIS framework defines a binary judgment for each statement in a model's output: is this statement fully attributable to the provided source material? NLI-based attribution evaluation automates the judgment of whether a passage entails a claim, but NLI models have their own error rates and cannot handle multi-hop inference.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Links: [{"title":"Natural Language Inference and Entailment Guide | AI Understanding","url":"https://aiunderstanding.org/learn/natural-language-inference-and-entailment"},{"title":"WiCE: Real-World Entailment for Claims in Wikipedia","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2303.01432"},{"title":"Natural Language Inference (NLI)","url":"https://www.emergentmind.com/topics/natural-language-inference-nli"},{"title":"Hallucination Detection: NLI, Self-Consistency & Learned Models - Interactive | Michael Brenndoerfer | Michael Brenndoerfer","url":"https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/hallucination-detection"},{"title":"Improving Evidence Retrieval with Claim-Evidence Entailment | NSF Public Access Repository","url":"https://par.nsf.gov/biblio/10378401-improving-evidence-retrieval-claim-evidence-entailment"},{"title":"BERT for Evidence Retrieval and Claim Verification","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/1910.02655"},{"title":"Minds versus Machines: Rethinking Entailment Verification with Language Models","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2402.03686v1"},{"title":"Detecting Corporate AI-Washing via Cross-Modal Semantic Inconsistency Learning","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.09644"},{"title":"soumyasanyal/nli-entailment-verifier-xxl · Hugging Face","url":"https://huggingface.co/soumyasanyal/nli-entailment-verifier-xxl"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about multi-premise entailment, NLI verification, and fact verification across multiple documents:

## Natural Language Inference for Claim Verification

Natural language inference (NLI), also called recognizing textual entailment, gives a model a premise and a hypothesis and asks for one of three labels: entailment (the hypothesis must be true given the premise), contradiction (it must be false), or neutral (it could be either).

## Multi-Document and Multi-Premise Applications

NLI is increasingly used as a building block rather than an end task: it powers automatic hallucination detection, where a generated claim is checked for entailment against source documents, and grounds retrieval-augmented systems. Document-level NLI enables automated verification of generated summaries or claims by determining if the salient hypothesis (summary, fact) is entailed by a source document.

## Handling Multiple Premises

Premises in this dataset typically contain multiple sentences, which is one of our focus areas. Additionally, this model is specifically trained to handle multi-sentence premises (similar to what we expect in CoT rationales and other modern LLM use cases).

## Fact Verification Frameworks

Claim verification is challenging because it requires first to find textual evidence and then apply claim-evidence entailment to verify a claim. Leveraging entailment prediction improves ranking multiple pieces of evidence.

The research demonstrates that NLI frameworks are being extended from single premise-hypothesis pairs to handle more complex scenarios involving multiple documents and multi-sentence premises for practical fact verification and claim validation tasks.

Web search results for query: "separating extractive claims from abstractive inferential claims faithfulness evaluation summarization "inferable" hedged"

Links: [{"title":"Extractive is not Faithful: An Investigation of Broad Unfaithfulness Problems in Extractive Summarization - ACL Anthology","url":"https://aclanthology.org/2023.acl-long.120/"},{"title":"FEQA: A Question Answering Evaluation Framework for Faithfulness   Assessment in Abstractive Summarization","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2005.03754"},{"title":"Faithful by Construction: Claim-Anchored Attribution for Multi-Document Summarization","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.23989"},{"title":"Faithful or Extractive? On Mitigating the Faithfulness-Abstractiveness   Trade-off in Abstractive Summarization","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2108.13684"},{"title":"FABLES: Evaluating faithfulness and content selection in book-length   summarization","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.01261"},{"title":"Faithfulness-Aware Decoding Strategies for Abstractive Summarization","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2303.03278"},{"title":"Extractive is not Faithful: An Investigation of Broad Unfaithfulness   Problems in Extractive Summarization","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2209.03549"},{"title":"Generating Faithful and Complete Hospital-Course Summaries from the   Electronic Health Record","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.01189"},{"title":"What are the Desired Characteristics of Calibration Sets? Identifying   Correlates on Long Form Scientific Summarization","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2305.07615"}]

I've found several research papers related to faithfulness evaluation in summarization, particularly examining the relationship between extractive and abstractive claims. Here's what the search revealed:

## Key Findings:

Research defines unfaithfulness problems in extractive summaries including incorrect coreference, incomplete coreference, incorrect discourse, incomplete discourse, and other misleading information. This is important because while extractive summarizers are largely faithful since they copy sentences from the source document, abstractive models struggle to produce faithful summaries without copying.

A framework for evaluating "effective faithfulness" has been proposed that takes into account the extractiveness of a system, recognizing that models can "trivially" become more faithful by becoming more extractive.

The research also addresses the tension between different types of claims. Evaluation has moved from n-gram overlap toward entailment- and QA-based consistency metrics and, most recently, decomposition into atomic facts.

Interestingly, research has analyzed extracted claims on summaries and manually evaluated the quality of extracted claims against the content of each summary, though this involves categorizing different types of statements including generic versus specific content.

The search results contain several academic papers from top-tier NLP venues addressing faithfulness evaluation in summarization with nuanced approaches to distinguishing types of claims and errors.

content.content:
Web search results for query: "Cochrane "informative statements" GRADE certainty hedged wording "probably reduces" "may reduce" plain language summary interpretation vs results"

Links: [{"title":"Chapter 14: Completing ‘Summary of findings’ tables and grading the certainty of the evidence | Cochrane","url":"https://www.cochrane.org/authors/handbooks-and-manuals/handbook/current/chapter-14"},{"title":"Chapter 14: Completing ‘Summary of findings’ tables and grading the certainty of the evidence | Cochrane Training","url":"https://training.cochrane.org/handbook/current/chapter-14"},{"title":"GRADE related publications | Cochrane","url":"https://www.cochrane.org/learn/courses-and-resources/cochrane-methodology/grade/grade-related-publications"},{"title":"GRADE | Cochrane","url":"https://www.cochrane.org/learn/courses-and-resources/cochrane-methodology/grade"},{"title":"GRADE guidelines 26: informative statements to communicate the findings of systematic reviews of interventions - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0895435619304160"},{"title":"Chapter 15: Interpreting results and drawing conclusions | Cochrane Training","url":"https://training.cochrane.org/handbook/current/chapter-15"},{"title":"guidance-writing-cochrane-plain-language-summary. ...","url":"https://www.cochrane.org/authors/handbooks-and-manuals/handbook/current/guidance-writing-cochrane-plain-language-summary.pdf"}]

Based on the search results, I found relevant information about GRADE informative statements and their wording.

The GRADE guidance provides statements for high, moderate and low certainty for a large effect on intervention x as: "x results in a large reduction…; x likely results in a large reduction…; x may result in a large reduction…," respectively. This illustrates how hedged wording is used to communicate different levels of certainty in evidence.

Producers and users of systematic reviews found statements to communicate findings combining size and certainty of an effect acceptable, with guidance and a wording template provided to formulate statements in systematic reviews and other decision tools.

Regarding plain language summaries, Cochrane guidance advises not to refer to evidence being 'downgraded', but instead to explain why one is less than confident in the results and focus on how the lack of confidence affects how one interprets the findings.

The search results show that most participants agreed statements should be based on size of effect and certainty of evidence, which underlies the systematic approach to using phrases like "probably reduces" and "may reduce" to convey both effect magnitude and evidence certainty to readers.

Links: [{"title":"Case-Based Deduction for Entailment Tree Generation","url":"https://www.mdpi.com/2227-7390/12/18/2893"},{"title":"[PDF] Explaining Answers with Entailment Trees | Semantic Scholar","url":"https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/Explaining-Answers-with-Entailment-Trees-Dalvi-Jansen/4a56f72b9c529810ba4ecfe9eac522d87f6db81d"},{"title":"Explaining Answers with Entailment Trees","url":"https://www.emergentmind.com/papers/2104.08661"},{"title":"Active entailment encoding for explanation tree construction using   parsimonious generation of hard negatives","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2208.01376"},{"title":"Entailment-Preserving First-order Logic Representations in Natural   Language Entailment","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2502.16757"},{"title":"Explaining Answers with Entailment Trees","url":"https://aclanthology.org/2021.emnlp-main.585.pdf"},{"title":"Explaining Answers with Entailment Trees - ACL Anthology","url":"https://aclanthology.org/2021.emnlp-main.585/"},{"title":"(PDF) Explaining Answers with Entailment Trees","url":"https://www.academia.edu/97512357/Explaining_Answers_with_Entailment_Trees"},{"title":"Exploring the Role of Reasoning Structures for Constructing Proofs in   Multi-Step Natural Language Reasoning with Large Language Models","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2410.08436"},{"title":"[2104.08661] Explaining Answers with Entailment Trees - ar5iv","url":"https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2104.08661"}]

Based on the search results, here's what EntailmentBank is about:

## Overview

EntailmentBank is the first dataset to contain multistep entailment trees. The approach generates explanations in the form of entailment trees, namely a tree of multipremise entailment steps from facts that are known, through intermediate conclusions, to the hypothesis of interest (namely the question + answer).

## Multi-Premise Reasoning

EntailmentBank's tasks involve reasoning from multiple premises to conclusions, which allows testing of multistep reasoning models. EntailmentBank contains larger inference problems, at a finer level of granularity than existing datasets, while being the only dataset to include multi-step entailments that make the reasoning steps explicit.

## Structure and Characteristics

In the entailment tree, the supporting evidence is the leaf node, the hypothesis is the root node, and the intermediate conclusions are the internal nodes. On average, each entailment tree includes 7.6 nodes across 3.2 entailment steps, where each entailment step typically involves 3 facts (two leaves, that combine to entail a conclusion). Discrete entailment steps in EntailmentBank require diverse forms of reasoning to solve, from forms of taxonomic or meronymic chaining (substitution) to application of domain-specific rules.

## Purpose

The goal is to explain answers by showing the line of reasoning from what is known to the answer, rather than simply showing a fragment of textual evidence (a "rationale").


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

input.prompt: Summarize: does this framework admit inference/abstractive claims not present in any single span? How does it verify cross-document claims? Any two-tier fact vs interpretation contract? Failure modes reported?

input.query: Claimify VeriScore unverifiable claims excluded factual precision "verifiable" vs "unverifiable" claim extraction decomposition

content.content:
# Summary: Claim-Anchored Attribution for Multi-Document Summarization

## Inference & Abstraction
The framework explicitly rejects inference. It operates under a **construction-by-design principle**: claims are "faithful by construction" because they are built directly from source spans. Per the paper, abstractive synthesis across documents is not permitted—all summary content must be anchored to explicit textual evidence.

## Cross-Document Verification
The framework handles multi-document claims through **span-level grounding across sources**. Claims integrating information from multiple documents are validated by ensuring each component attaches to a verifiable span, though the paper does not detail a separate cross-document reconciliation mechanism beyond per-span attribution.

## Fact vs. Interpretation Contract
No explicit two-tier contract is reported. The framework treats all summarization output uniformly: each claim requires direct span anchoring. There is no separate handling for factual assertions versus interpretive statements.

## Reported Failure Modes
The paper mentions several limitations:
- **Decontextualization risk**: spans may become misleading when removed from original context
- **Semantic divergence**: "claims may drift from source meaning despite textual anchoring"
- **Limited scope**: the approach cannot capture inferences, nuance, or implicit information in source texts

No quantitative failure rate analysis is provided in the PDF excerpt.

[Binary content (application/pdf, 413.9KB) also saved to /home/polaris/.claude/projects/-home-polaris-polaris-project/21e87760-8436-4090-870d-99ef2121882e/tool-results/webfetch-1783922147190-hp1ao4.pdf]

Web search results for query: "Claimify VeriScore unverifiable claims excluded factual precision "verifiable" vs "unverifiable" claim extraction decomposition"

Links: [{"title":"VeriScore: Evaluating the factuality of verifiable claims in long-form text generation","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2406.19276v1"},{"title":"Claimify: Extracting high-quality claims from language model outputs - Microsoft Research","url":"https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/blog/claimify-extracting-high-quality-claims-from-language-model-outputs/"},{"title":"Towards Effective Extraction and Evaluation of Factual ...","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2502.10855"},{"title":"[Literature Review] Towards Effective Extraction and Evaluation of Factual Claims","url":"https://www.themoonlight.io/en/review/towards-effective-extraction-and-evaluation-of-factual-claims"},{"title":"VERISCORE: Evaluating the factuality of verifiable claims in long-form text generation [Quick Review]","url":"https://liner.com/review/veriscore-evaluating-factuality-verifiable-claims-in-longform-text-generation"},{"title":"Towards Effective Extraction and Evaluation of Factual Claims - Microsoft Research","url":"https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/publication/towards-effective-extraction-and-evaluation-of-factual-claims/"},{"title":"[2406.19276] VERISCORE: Evaluating the factuality of verifiable claims in long-form text generation","url":"https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.19276"},{"title":"(PDF) Towards Effective Extraction and Evaluation of Factual Claims","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/389091379_Towards_Effective_Extraction_and_Evaluation_of_Factual_Claims"},{"title":"VeriScore: Evaluating the factuality of verifiable claims in long-form text generation - ACL Anthology","url":"https://aclanthology.org/2024.findings-emnlp.552/"},{"title":"VeriScore: Evaluating Factuality Across Domains","url":"https://www.emergentmind.com/topics/veriscore"}]

Based on the search results, I found comprehensive information about both Claimify and VeriScore, two key systems for claim extraction and factuality evaluation:

## Overview

VeriScore is a metric for diverse long-form generation tasks that contain both verifiable and unverifiable content. Claimify is a novel LLM-based method that outperforms prior solutions for extracting and evaluating factual claims.

## Key Difference: Handling Unverifiable Content

A central distinction between these approaches and earlier methods is their treatment of unverifiable claims. Existing metrics like FActScore and SAFE decompose text into "atomic claims" and assume every claim is verifiable (i.e., can plausibly be proven true or false), which is not suitable for most generation tasks.

Many long-form outputs interleave factual claims with unverifiable content such as hypotheticals. Since FActScore and SAFE assume all claims are verifiable, they extract everything from the text (including unverifiable content like examples or hypotheticals), which can unfairly penalize models during the final aggregation process.

## Claim Extraction and Decomposition

VeriScore introduces a novel claim extraction approach that exclusively identifies verifiable claims, unlike prior methods that decompose all content indiscriminately. VeriScore integrates inter-sentence context during claim extraction to ensure self-contained claims and resolve referents.

Claimify is the first claim extraction system that identifies when source text has multiple possible interpretations. Specifically, Claimify outperforms existing methods by ensuring 99% of claims extracted are entailed by their source sentence, striking the best balance between including verifiable content and excluding unverifiable content.

## Performance Comparison

VeriScore's claim extraction method significantly outperforms SAFE and FActScore being favored 93% of the time, stemming from its ability to extract only verifiable claims and its consideration of inter-sentence context, which resolves issues like vague references and the extraction of unverifiable content.

Links: [{"title":"(PDF) Automation of Systematic Reviews with Large Language Models","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/392665467_Automation_of_Systematic_Reviews_with_Large_Language_Models"},{"title":"Accelerating clinical evidence synthesis with large language models | npj Digital Medicine","url":"https://www.nature.com/articles/s41746-025-01840-7"},{"title":"Automation of Systematic Reviews with Large Language Models","url":"https://ottosr.com/manuscript.pdf"},{"title":"Automation of Systematic Reviews with Large Language Models","url":"https://www.medrxiv.org/content/10.1101/2025.06.13.25329541.full.pdf"},{"title":"Automation of Systematic Reviews with Large Language Models | medRxiv","url":"https://www.medrxiv.org/content/10.1101/2025.06.13.25329541v1.full"},{"title":"Accelerating clinical evidence synthesis with large language models - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12331930/"},{"title":"Large language models for conducting systematic reviews: on the rise, but not yet ready for use—a scoping review - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0895435625000794"}]

I've completed the web search for your query on LLM-based systematic review automation tools. Here's a summary of the findings:

## Key Tools and Findings

**Otto-SR:** An end-to-end agentic workflow using large language models (LLMs) to support and automate the SR workflow from initial search to analysis. Otto-SR outperformed traditional dual human workflows in SR screening (otto-SR: 96.7% sensitivity, 97.9% specificity; human: 81.7% sensitivity, 98.1% specificity) and data extraction (otto-SR: 93.1% accuracy; human: 79.7% accuracy).

**TrialMind:** An AI-driven pipeline optimized for generating boolean queries to search citations from the literature, building eligibility criteria and screening through the found citations, and extracting data from publications and reports. TrialMind breaks down into subtasks that adhere to the established practice of systematic reviews, which facilitates experts in the loop to monitor, edit, and verify intermediate outputs.

**Addressing Hallucination and Safeguards:** Research aims to overcome LLM's limitations in hallucinations, weakness in reasoning with numerical data, overly generic outputs, and lack of transparency and reliability.

**Elicit:** The search results reference Elicit as a commercial LLM-based systematic review automation software used in comparative evaluations, though limited details about its specific safeguards are provided.

**RobotReviewer:** Unlike ML tools like RobotReviewer, LLMs do not rely on specialized algorithms and are not originally designed to process structured metadata or ensure transparent reproducibility.

Human-in-the-loop approaches and expert verification of intermediate outputs appear to be key safeguards discussed in the literature.

Web search results for query: "hedging epistemic markers LLM overreliance "hedged" hallucination still misleads users uncertainty expressions trust"

Links: [{"title":"Humans overrely on overconfident language models, across languages","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2507.06306"},{"title":"Can LLMs Use Linguistic Uncertainty Markers to Reliably Reflect Intrinsic Confidence?","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.28778"},{"title":"Why Does Self-Distillation (Sometimes) Degrade the Reasoning Capability of LLMs?","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.24472"},{"title":"1 Beyond Accuracy: Rethinking Hallucination and Regulatory Response in","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2509.13345"},{"title":"Can LLMs Use Linguistic Uncertainty Markers to Reliably Reflect Intrinsic Confidence?","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2605.28778"},{"title":"Revisiting Epistemic Markers in Confidence Estimation: Can Markers Accurately Reflect Large Language Models' Uncertainty?","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24778"},{"title":"Saying More Than They Know: A Framework for Quantifying Epistemic-Rhetorical Miscalibration in Large Language Models","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.19768"},{"title":"To Believe or Not to Believe Your LLM","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2406.02543"},{"title":"Uncertainty-Based Abstention in LLMs Improves Safety and Reduces   Hallucinations","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.10960"}]

Based on the search results, here's what research shows about hedging epistemic markers, LLM overreliance, and their relationship to hallucinations and user trust:

## Key Findings

**LLMs Are Overconfident Despite Using Uncertainty Markers**

LLMs are overconfident across languages, frequently generating strengtheners even as part of incorrect responses. This creates a critical problem: LLMs continue to suffer from hallucinations, wherein false claims are habitually conveyed using decisive, assertive language.

**Hedged Responses Still Mislead Users**

The use of hedging phrases doesn't necessarily protect users. Over- or under-confident generations are able to mislead users, undermine trust, and foster over-reliance in LLM-based systems. Even more concerning, participants are significantly more likely to discount expressions of uncertainty in Japanese than in English (i.e., ignore their 'hedging' function and rely on generations that contain them).

**Fundamental Misalignment Between Markers and Actual Confidence**

Research reveals systemic problems with epistemic markers: LLMs show systematic deficiencies in their ability to faithfully express their intrinsic confidence in words. Furthermore, marker confidence shifts significantly under distribution changes, and models struggle to effectively differentiate between markers and maintain consistent marker rankings across datasets.

**Trust and Overreliance Risks**

Overreliance risks are high across languages when studying multilingual linguistic miscalibration, overconfidence, and overreliance, suggesting this is a widespread issue affecting global LLM safety.

Links: [{"title":"Explicit Evidence Grounding via Structured Inline Citation Generation","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2606.07130"},{"title":"ScholarCopilot: Training Large Language Models for Academic Writing with   Accurate Citations","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2504.00824"},{"title":"FACTUM: Mechanistic Detection of Citation Hallucination in Long-Form RAG","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2601.05866"},{"title":"CRUD-RAG: A Comprehensive Chinese Benchmark for Retrieval-Augmented   Generation of Large Language Models","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2401.17043"},{"title":"Explicit Evidence Grounding via Structured Inline Citation Generation","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2606.07130"},{"title":"Reason and Verify: A Framework for Faithful Retrieval-Augmented Generation","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2603.10143"},{"title":"CogCanvas: Verbatim-Grounded Artifact Extraction for Long LLM Conversations","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2601.00821v2"},{"title":"SemanticCite: Citation Verification with AI-Powered Full-Text Analysis and Evidence-Based Reasoning","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2511.16198"},{"title":"Detecting and Correcting Reference Hallucinations in Commercial LLMs and Deep Research Agents","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.03173"}]

Based on the search results, I found several relevant arXiv papers related to citations and RAG systems, though the specific combination of all those exact terms doesn't appear to have a single primary result. Here are the most relevant findings:

The closest match appears to be research on **citation attribution and verification in RAG systems**:

1. FullCite is a structured verbatim inline citations approach that facilitates reliable attribution for RAG-based QA, requiring that every claim in the answer is followed by a citation comprising the identifier of the source document and a verbatim evidence span from the selected document.

2. Citation hallucinations in RAG models represent a deceptive failure where a model cites a source that fails to support its claim.

3. One framework introduces a verification taxonomy that distinguishes between explicit and implicit support patterns to facilitate structured error diagnosis of rationale faithfulness.

4. Research categorizes critical failure modes by distinguishing between implicit and explicit support patterns.

The papers I found focus on verbatim grounding, citation hallucinations, and distinguishing between explicit versus implicit support for claims in RAG systems. However, I didn't find a paper with all those specific keywords together. If you're looking for a particular paper, you might want to try a more targeted arXiv search with a specific title or author name.

Based on the search results, here are the relevant GitHub repositories and information for multi-hop fact verification datasets and models:

## Key Repositories Found:

1. **MultiVerS (dwadden/multivers)** - MultiVerS is trained on FEVER and scientific datasets, with access to CovidFact, HealthVer, and SciFact datasets. MultiVerS achieves state-of-the-art on SciFact and two other scientific claim verification datasets.

4. **EX-FEVER (dependentsign/EX-FEVER)** - A dataset for multi-hop explainable fact verification from ACL 2024.

## Key Datasets Mentioned:

- **HoVer**: A dataset for multi-hop fact verification that challenges models to extract relevant evidence from several Wikipedia articles and verify whether the claim is SUPPORTED or REFUTED.
- **SciFact**: Scientific claim verification dataset with evidence sets
- **FEVER**: A large-scale fact verification dataset with claims generated by altering sentences extracted from Wikipedia, where more than 87% only require information from a single Wikipedia article.

Links: [{"title":"Large Language Model Sourcing: A Survey","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.10161"},{"title":"GitHub - princeton-nlp/ALCE: [EMNLP 2023] Enabling Large Language Models to Generate Text with Citations. Paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.14627 · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/princeton-nlp/ALCE"},{"title":"[Quick Review] Enabling Large Language Models to Generate Text with Citations","url":"https://liner.com/review/enabling-large-language-models-to-generate-text-with-citations"},{"title":"Efficient Citer: Tuning Large Language Models for ...","url":"https://aclanthology.org/2024.findings-naacl.277.pdf"},{"title":"Enabling Large Language Models to Generate Text with ...","url":"https://aclanthology.org/2023.emnlp-main.398.pdf"},{"title":"[2305.14627] ALCE: An Automatic Benchmark for Large Language Model Generations with Citations","url":"https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/2305.14627"},{"title":"Enabling Large Language Models to Generate Text with Citations Tianyu Gao","url":"https://openreview.net/pdf?id=bxFwIn0wZ0"},{"title":"ALCE Benchmark: Citation-Grounded Text Generation","url":"https://www.emergentmind.com/papers/2305.14627"},{"title":"[2305.14627] Enabling Large Language Models to Generate Text with Citations","url":"https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.14627"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about ALCE and its approach to citation evaluation:

## ALCE: Enabling Large Language Models to Generate Text with Citations

ALCE is the first benchmark for Automatic LLMs' Citation Evaluation that collects a diverse set of questions and retrieval corpora and requires building end-to-end systems to retrieve supporting evidence and generate answers with citations.

### Citation Quality Metrics

ALCE formalizes citation recall and citation precision in an automatic setting by leveraging the NLI model TRUE to determine whether cited passages entail the generated content.

The evaluation approach is based on the following methodology:

- **Citation Recall**: For each statement, its citation recall is 1 if and only if there is at least one citation and the concatenation of cited passages entails the statement.

- **Citation Precision**: Citation precision identifies any irrelevant citations.

### Evaluation Performance

For citation recall, ALCE achieves an accuracy of 66.1%—ALCE is effective in detecting "irrelevant" citations, but due to the limitation of the NLI model (cannot detect "partial support"), it has a relatively high false positive rate.

The NLI model used in ALCE evaluation is specifically designed for this task, and the methodology relies on checking whether the concatenation of cited passages can entail the generated statements through natural language inference.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Web search results for query: ""Attribute First, then Generate" OR "Attribute or Abstain" attribution long documents unattributable sentences abstain"

Links: [{"title":"Attribute First, then Generate: Locally-attributable Grounded Text Generation - ACL Anthology","url":"https://aclanthology.org/2024.acl-long.182/"},{"title":"Attribute First, then Generate: Locally-attributable Grounded Text Generation","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2403.17104"},{"title":"[2403.17104] Attribute First, then Generate: Locally-attributable Grounded Text Generation","url":"https://arxiv.org/abs/2403.17104"},{"title":"GitHub - UKPLab/emnlp2024-attribute-or-abstain: This repository contains the code for the paper \"Attribute or Abstain: LLMs as Long Document Assistants\", published on arxiv. · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/UKPLab/emnlp2024-attribute-or-abstain"},{"title":"Attribute or Abstain: Large Language Models as Long Document Assistants","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2407.07799"},{"title":"Attribute First, then Generate: Locally-attributable Grounded Text   Generation","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2403.17104"},{"title":"Attribute or Abstain: Large Language Models as Long Document Assistants","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2407.07799"},{"title":"[2407.07799] Attribute or Abstain: Large Language Models as Long Document Assistants","url":"https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.07799"}]

I found relevant research on both topics you searched for. Here are the key findings:

## "Attribute First, then Generate"

This method breaks down the conventional end-to-end generation process into three intuitive steps: content selection, sentence planning, and sequential sentence generation. By initially identifying relevant source segments ("select first") and then conditioning the generation process on them ("then generate"), these segments also act as the output's fine-grained attributions ("select" becomes "attribute").

The method was tested on Multi-document Summarization and Long-form Question-answering, yielding more concise citations than baselines while maintaining - and in some cases enhancing - both generation quality and attribution accuracy. The approach yields orders of magnitude shorter attributions, shortening the fact-checking time by almost 50%, with little impact on the attribution accuracy.

## "Attribute or Abstain"

This research addresses attribution for long documents. The work considers generating a response containing statements, with the option to abstain (e.g., by saying "unanswerable"), and retrieving evidence for each statement such that it is attributable to that evidence.

The paper defines different attribution approaches: (1) post-hoc, (2) retrieve-then-read, and (3) citation; with two additional approaches (4) reduced-post-hoc and (5) reduced-citation using an initial retrieval step to decrease input length.

Both papers address the challenge of providing fine-grained, verifiable attributions for generated text, particularly in the context of long documents.

Links: [{"title":"AI Newsletter Series (Part 2): Designing a Useful LLM Pipeline Across PDFs, Use Cases, and Accounts","url":"https://community.databricks.com/t5/technical-blog/ai-newsletter-series-part-2-designing-a-useful-llm-pipeline/ba-p/145873"},{"title":"LLM-MedQA: Enhancing Medical Question Answering through Case Studies in   Large Language Models","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2501.05464"},{"title":"Harnessing LLM Ensembles for KG-Grounded Narrative Extraction: Disinformation vs. Trustworthy News","url":"https://www.mdpi.com/2076-3417/16/4/1962"},{"title":"LOGOS: LLM-DRIVEN END-TO-END GROUNDED THEORY DEVELOPMENT AND SCHEMA INDUCTION","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2509.24294"},{"title":"Knowledge Graphs + LLMs: A Potential Mental Model for AI Architecture","url":"https://www.fortegrp.com/insights/knowledge-graphs-as-fact-tables-llms-as-dimensions-a-potential-mental-model-for-ai-architecture"},{"title":"A Unified Evaluation and Governance Framework for Trustworthy ...","url":"https://www.techrxiv.org/doi/pdf/10.36227/techrxiv.176799772.28164151/v1"},{"title":"The FACTS Grounding Leaderboard: Benchmarking LLMs ...","url":"https://storage.googleapis.com/deepmind-media/FACTS/FACTS_grounding_paper.pdf"},{"title":"From Detection to Response: A Deep Learning and Retrieval-Augmented Generation Framework for Network Intrusion Mitigation","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.17960"},{"title":"Autonomous Construction-Site Safety Inspection Using Mobile Robots: A Multilayer VLM-LLM Pipeline","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2512.13974"},{"title":"RAG-KT: Cross-platform Explainable Knowledge Tracing with Multi-view Fusion Retrieval Generation","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.10960"}]

Based on the search results, I found several relevant design patterns and approaches for separating grounded facts from interpretation/analysis in LLM report generation:

## Key Design Patterns

**Separation of Structured vs. Unstructured Data:**
Structured data (like system-of-record fields and timestamps) should define what is true, while unstructured data provides supporting evidence. By explicitly combining unstructured evidence with structured context, the system constrains the model to generate outputs that reflect both historical grounding and current state.

**Knowledge Graph + LLM Architecture:**
Query the knowledge graph first for authoritative facts, then use LLMs to interpret and contextualize those results. This ensures responses are grounded in verified data while remaining conversational and accessible. The beauty of this separation is clear: facts remain stable and queryable, while dimensions provide rich, flexible context for interpretation.

**Multi-Stage Pipeline with Separation of Concerns:**
LLMs tend to perform best when focused on a single reasoning task at a time rather than handling multistep visual reasoning and long-form summarization simultaneously. By isolating report generation, the framework allows earlier modules to specialize in perception and rule grounding, while reserving the LLM for synthesizing coherent narratives from structured inputs.

**Controlled Merging of Evidence:**
The pattern is to keep evidence and context separate, and only merge them at controlled points in the generation flow.

**Ensemble-Based Contradiction Checking:**
Ensemble policies can include recall-oriented union that retained all model outputs and precision-oriented consensus that kept only agreed elements, plus an LLM critic that flagged missing links, contradictions and framing inconsistencies.

These patterns help prevent hallucination while maintaining interpretability by ensuring facts remain verifiable and distinct from interpretive layers.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

findings: VERDICT: The two-tier contract is HALF prior art and HALF novel — and the novel half is the half that does not hold. Tier 1 (FACT, span-grounded, entailment-judged) is standard (AIS / Rashkin et al., TACL-CL 2023). Tier 2 as you specify it (hedged, uncited, contradiction-screened-only) has NO named precedent in NLG/RAG attribution research, because the field explicitly refuses to give a generation-side license to unentailed content. What the field does instead is either (a) verify inference against a SET of premises, or (b) exempt unverifiable content from SCORING while never claiming it is safe. You are conflating (b) with a safety guarantee. Contradiction-screening yields 'not-refuted', which is strictly weaker than 'entailed': NLI-neutral is exactly where fabrication lives, so tier 2 as written provably does NOT guarantee no fabrication — it defines a sanctioned channel for it.

THE REAL PRIOR ART YOU WANT — 'entailment from a set of premises' is a solved, named, implemented pattern. Do not invent a new tier; extend the judge's premise. (1) ALCE (Gao et al., EMNLP 2023, princeton-nlp/ALCE): citation recall = 1 iff the CONCATENATION of all cited passages entails the statement, scored with the TRUE NLI model. This is literally 'multi-premise entailment over the union of cited spans' and it is the standard metric for citation-bearing long-form generation. (2) FEVER's 'evidence sets' and HoVer (many-hop, up to 4 Wikipedia articles) define SUPPORTED as entailed by a UNION of sentences from multiple documents. (3) MultiVerS / SciFact (allenai/scifact, dwadden/multivers) verify scientific claims against multi-sentence, multi-abstract rationales. (4) EntailmentBank (Dalvi et al., EMNLP 2021) formalizes multi-premise entailment TREES: leaves = source facts, internal nodes = intermediate conclusions, root = the inference. A cross-source insight sentence is exactly an entailment-tree root over spans from >1 source. IMPLICATION FOR POLARIS: your judge's 'NOT present in the SPAN => NEUTRAL => DELETED' rule is not a constraint of entailment verification — it is an artifact of passing ONE span as premise. Change the premise from a span to the union of the cited spans (plus, if needed, a licensed background axiom set) and a genuine cross-source inference becomes ENTAILED, not NEUTRAL. This recovers the insight sentence type WITHOUT weakening the guarantee, and it is the change I would make before building any hedge tier.

TIER-2's CLOSEST NLP ANCESTORS, and why they are not a license: VeriScore (Song et al., Findings EMNLP 2024) and Claimify (Metropolitansky & Larson, MSR 2025, 'Towards Effective Extraction and Evaluation of Factual Claims') both note that FActScore/SAFE wrongly assume every decomposed claim is verifiable, and both therefore EXCLUDE unverifiable content (hypotheticals, subjective statements, opinions) from claim extraction and from the factuality score. Claimify additionally refuses to extract claims from sentences with multiple possible interpretations and reports 99% of extracted claims are entailed by their source sentence. Note the asymmetry: they exclude unverifiable content from the METRIC; they never assert the unverifiable content in the output is safe. If POLARIS adopts the same move (a class of sentences the entailment judge skips), you inherit the exemption WITHOUT inheriting any guarantee. That is the precise gap in your contract.

THE ONE PLACE THE TWO-TIER CONTRACT REALLY EXISTS IS EVIDENCE SYNTHESIS — and it is enforced socially, not mechanically. Cochrane/GRADE is your genuine prior art and it is worth naming in the design doc: (i) a hard structural split between Results (data extraction; every number traceable to a study; Cochrane Handbook ch.14 'Summary of findings' tables) and Discussion/Interpretation (Handbook ch.15, 'Interpreting results and drawing conclusions'), where the latter may not introduce new data; (ii) GRADE guidelines 26 (Santesso et al., J Clin Epidemiol 2020), 'informative statements to communicate the findings of systematic reviews' — a WORDING TEMPLATE that mechanically keys the hedge to the certainty rating: 'X reduces...' (high) / 'X likely/probably reduces...' (moderate) / 'X may reduce...' (low) / 'the evidence is very uncertain' (very low). That is the mature version of your INTERPRETATION tier: the hedge is not free-form authorial style, it is a closed vocabulary selected by a computed certainty score, and the interpretive statement must be about the SAME quantity that was extracted (no new entity, no new mechanism). Also: Cochrane plain-language-summary guidance forbids introducing methodological jargon and requires that lack of confidence be explained rather than asserted. Two-tier is standard practice in systematic reviews; what makes it hold there is (a) a fixed inference schema, (b) certainty-keyed hedge vocabulary, (c) human editorial peer review — NOT an automated screen.

LLM SYSTEMATIC-REVIEW TOOLS DO NOT SOLVE THIS — they avoid it. otto-SR (medRxiv 2025.06.13.25329541; 96.7% screening sensitivity, 93.1% extraction accuracy vs 79.7% human) and TrialMind (npj Digital Medicine 2025) both decompose into SCREENING + EXTRACTION + statistical aggregation, i.e. they automate the span-grounded tier and leave the synthesis/interpretation tier to humans-in-the-loop with expert verification of intermediate outputs. A 2025 scoping review (J Clin Epidemiol, 'LLMs for conducting systematic reviews: on the rise, but not yet ready for use') is explicit that the reasoning/interpretation step is not yet trusted. So: no shipped evidence-synthesis system today automates a fabrication-safe interpretation tier. If you build one, you are ahead of that field, not behind it — which means no borrowed safety argument is available to you.

MULTI-DOCUMENT SUMMARISATION HAS ALREADY TRIED AND ABANDONED YOUR PROBLEM. 'Faithful by Construction: Claim-Anchored Attribution for Multi-Document Summarization' (arXiv 2606.23989) takes the strict route — every claim anchored to an explicit span, abstractive cross-document synthesis NOT PERMITTED — and its own limitations section concedes the approach 'cannot capture inferences, nuance, or implicit information', plus decontextualization risk (a span that misleads once removed from context) and semantic drift despite anchoring. 'Attribute First, then Generate' (Slobodkin et al., ACL 2024) improves attribution granularity by selecting spans first, then generating conditioned on them — same trade, insight sacrificed for locality. 'Attribute or Abstain' (Buchmann et al., EMNLP 2024, UKPLab/emnlp2024-attribute-or-abstain) formalizes the only two options the field currently sanctions for an unattributable statement: attribute it, or abstain. There is no third 'assert it hedged' option in the literature. That absence is the answer to your 'is it novel' question: it is novel, and it is novel because everyone else concluded it was unsound.

FAILURE MODES OF THE HEDGE TIER (these are documented, and they are fatal to the safety claim): (1) HEDGES DO NOT NEUTRALIZE FALSE CONTENT. 'Humans overrely on overconfident language models, across languages' (arXiv 2507.06306) shows readers discount hedging function and rely on the content anyway (strongly in some languages). A hedged fabricated mechanism is still a fabricated mechanism the reader will carry away. Your contract's safety argument implicitly assumes hedging transfers epistemic risk to the reader; the HCI evidence says it does not. (2) THE HEDGE IS NOT CALIBRATED. 'Revisiting Epistemic Markers in Confidence Estimation' (arXiv 2505.24778) and follow-ups show LLM linguistic uncertainty markers do not track intrinsic confidence, shift under distribution change, and are not consistently ranked — so 'may indicate' in a generated sentence carries no information about whether the inference is sound. (3) NEUTRAL != FALSE-SAFE. Contradiction-screening only catches inferences the sources actively refute; the entire fabrication surface (a plausible mechanism no source discusses) is NEUTRAL, passes the screen, and ships. (4) NLI JUDGES ARE WEAK EXACTLY ON MULTI-PREMISE INPUTS. ALCE itself reports its NLI-based citation-recall judge is only ~66% accurate and cannot detect PARTIAL support (high false-positive rate); 'Minds versus Machines: Rethinking Entailment Verification with Language Models' (Sanyal et al. 2024; soumyasanyal/nli-entailment-verifier-xxl) shows off-the-shelf NLI models degrade on multi-sentence premises and that LLM-based verifiers are needed. So if you go the multi-premise route (which you should), budget for a purpose-built verifier and expect the judge itself to be the weakest link. (5) DECONTEXTUALIZATION: WiCE (Kamoi et al., EMNLP 2023) shows real-world claims are frequently only PARTIALLY supported and require decomposition into sub-claims — a cross-source sentence that is 60% supported will read as 100% supported.

CONCRETE RECOMMENDATION (what I would test, in priority order): (A) TIER 1.5 BEFORE TIER 2 — before admitting any unverified content, re-run the judge with premise = concatenation of the N cited spans (ALCE's exact protocol). Measure what fraction of currently-DELETED 'insight' sentences flip from NEUTRAL to ENTAILED. My prediction: a large fraction of the sentences you think need a hedge tier are actually multi-premise-entailed and were only killed by the one-span premise. This is a cheap experiment and it may dissolve the problem. (B) LICENSED INFERENCE SCHEMAS, NOT FREE-FORM INTERPRETATION — if a residue remains, do not admit free-form hedged prose. Admit a CLOSED SET of cross-source relations computable from structured claim tuples: agreement, disagreement/contradiction between sources, evidence gap ('no source in the corpus addresses X'), magnitude/temporal comparison, population/scope mismatch. Each is deterministically checkable against the extracted tuples, so it is grounded BY CONSTRUCTION and needs no hedge to be true. GRADE-26 is the precedent for keying the wording to a computed certainty. (C) HEDGE VOCABULARY MUST BE CLOSED AND SCORE-KEYED, never model-chosen. (D) If you ship a genuinely free tier-2, treat it as an UNGUARANTEED region and label it as such in the artifact (a visual/structural separation, as Cochrane separates Results from Discussion) — do not let its sentences be visually interleaved with tier-1 sentences, because the reader's fabrication risk is dominated by their inability to tell the tiers apart. (E) TEST HARNESS: your existing entailment judge cannot evaluate tier 2 by construction, so build an adversarial eval — inject known-false mechanisms that are consistent-with-but-unsupported-by the corpus and measure the contradiction screen's catch rate. I expect it to be near zero; that number is the honest fabrication rate of the contract.
input.evidence:
PRIMARY SOURCES (all verified via search/fetch this session):
- AIS / "Measuring Attribution in Natural Language Generation Models" (Rashkin et al., Computational Linguistics 49(4) 2023) — https://direct.mit.edu/coli/article/49/4/777/116438/Measuring-Attribution-in-Natural-Language ; repo https://github.com/google-research-datasets/AIS
- ALCE, "Enabling LLMs to Generate Text with Citations" (Gao et al., EMNLP 2023) — https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.14627 ; repo https://github.com/princeton-nlp/ALCE ; citation recall = concatenation-of-cited-passages entails the statement (TRUE NLI); judge accuracy ~66%, cannot detect partial support
- EntailmentBank, "Explaining Answers with Entailment Trees" (Dalvi et al., EMNLP 2021) — https://aclanthology.org/2021.emnlp-main.585/ ; multi-premise entailment trees, avg 3.2 steps, ~3 facts per step
- HoVer (many-hop claim verification over up to 4 Wikipedia articles) and FEVER evidence sets — index: https://github.com/Cartus/Automated-Fact-Checking-Resources
- SciFact / MultiVerS (multi-abstract scientific claim verification) — https://github.com/allenai/scifact , https://github.com/dwadden/multivers
- WiCE, "Real-World Entailment for Claims in Wikipedia" (partial support, sub-claim decomposition) — https://arxiv.org/pdf/2303.01432
- "Minds versus Machines: Rethinking Entailment Verification with Language Models" (Sanyal et al. 2024) — https://arxiv.org/html/2402.03686v1 ; model https://huggingface.co/soumyasanyal/nli-entailment-verifier-xxl (explicitly trained for MULTI-SENTENCE premises)
- VeriScore (Song et al., Findings EMNLP 2024) — https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.19276 ; extracts ONLY verifiable claims, excludes unverifiable content from scoring
- Claimify / "Towards Effective Extraction and Evaluation of Factual Claims" (Microsoft Research 2025) — https://arxiv.org/pdf/2502.10855 ; https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/blog/claimify-extracting-high-quality-claims-from-language-model-outputs/ ; 99% of extracted claims entailed by source sentence; refuses ambiguous sentences
- "Attribute First, then Generate" (Slobodkin et al., ACL 2024) — https://arxiv.org/abs/2403.17104
- "Attribute or Abstain: LLMs as Long Document Assistants" (EMNLP 2024) — https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.07799 ; repo https://github.com/UKPLab/emnlp2024-attribute-or-abstain
- "Faithful by Construction: Claim-Anchored Attribution for Multi-Document Summarization" (arXiv 2606.23989) — fetched; explicitly forbids abstractive cross-document synthesis; limitations concede it "cannot capture inferences, nuance, or implicit information", plus decontextualization + semantic drift
- Survey: "A Survey of Large Language Models Attribution" (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2311.03731) and https://github.com/HITsz-TMG/awesome-llm-attributions — no "inference-level attribution" tier is defined anywhere in the taxonomy; attribution is uniformly source-verifiability
EVIDENCE-SYNTHESIS / SYSTEMATIC REVIEW:
- Cochrane Handbook ch.14 (Summary of findings + GRADE) — https://training.cochrane.org/handbook/current/chapter-14 ; ch.15 (Interpreting results and drawing conclusions) — https://training.cochrane.org/handbook/current/chapter-15 — the structural Results/Interpretation split
- GRADE guidelines 26: informative statements (Santesso et al., J Clin Epidemiol 2020) — https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0895435619304160 — certainty-keyed hedge template: "reduces" / "likely reduces" / "may reduce" / "very uncertain"
- otto-SR (medRxiv 2025) — https://www.medrxiv.org/content/10.1101/2025.06.13.25329541v1.full — automates screening+extraction only
- TrialMind (npj Digital Medicine 2025) — https://www.nature.com/articles/s41746-025-01840-7 — human-in-the-loop verification of intermediate outputs
- "LLMs for conducting systematic reviews: on the rise, but not yet ready for use" (J Clin Epidemiol 2025) — https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0895435625000794
HEDGE-TIER FAILURE-MODE EVIDENCE:
- "Humans overrely on overconfident language models, across languages" — https://arxiv.org/html/2507.06306 (readers discount hedges)
- "Revisiting Epistemic Markers in Confidence Estimation" — https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24778 (markers do not track intrinsic confidence; unstable under distribution shift)
- "Can LLMs Use Linguistic Uncertainty Markers to Reliably Reflect Intrinsic Confidence?" — https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.28778
NEGATIVE RESULT: targeted searches for "inference-level attribution", "reasoning attribution" as a provenance tier, and for any generation-side contract admitting hedged-uncited-unentailed sentences returned NOTHING matching. The terms "inference-level attribution"/"reasoning attribution" in the literature mean failure attribution in multi-agent systems or inference-TIME (as opposed to training-time) attribution — a different sense entirely. No prior art names your tier-2. It is novel, and the surrounding literature's uniform choice of "attribute or abstain" is evidence that it was considered and rejected as unsound rather than overlooked.
exploitable: Multi-premise entailment (premise = concatenation of the cited spans, ALCE protocol) — legitimate, standard, and recovers most cross-source insight sentences without weakening the guarantee

Licensed inference schemas over structured claim tuples (agreement / disagreement / evidence gap / magnitude comparison / scope mismatch) — grounded by construction, checkable deterministically, no hedge needed
Entailment-tree-style intermediate conclusions: emit the inference plus the 2-3 span premises it composes, so the judge verifies the STEP rather than the sentence in isolation
GRADE-26-style closed, certainty-keyed hedge vocabulary (reduces / likely reduces / may reduce / very uncertain) instead of model-chosen free-form hedging
Cochrane-style structural separation of Results from Interpretation in the rendered artifact, so an unguaranteed region is never visually interleaved with span-grounded sentences
